abstract:
During his premiership, Tony Blair was famously satirized by *Private Eye* as the "Revd. A.R.P. Blair," a trendy vicar delivering earnest homilies from the pulpit of "St. Albion’s." This caricature worked because the public recognized that Christian faith was a cornerstone of Blair’s identity, even as it remained his most enigmatic political trait. While he was vocal about his beliefs while leading the Opposition, Blair became notably more diffident once in power, surrounded by advisors whose mantra was famously "we don’t do God."

Despite the obvious influence of religion on Blair—and now on Gordon Brown, who frequently cites his Church of Scotland upbringing—political commentators have largely failed to treat this dimension with serious academic rigor. It is a startling omission that suggests secular analysts are often as uncomfortable with "doing God" as the political spin doctors themselves. This lack of informed attention "brackets out" a vital force in British public life.

This chapter argues that the role of faith in New Labour’s project must be analyzed as a matter of public discourse rather than mere private disposition. The "remoralizing" of Britain—from ethical foreign policy and social inclusion to the "war on terror"—has relied heavily on a moral vocabulary rooted in the heritage of Christian socialism. Yet, this resurgence of religion in the public square stands in direct tension with a British culture where religious participation is at a historic low and the "secularization thesis" has long been the dominant narrative.

By examining the shifting boundaries between the public and the private, this chapter critiques the conventional wisdom that liberal democracy requires a value-free, secular domain. Instead, it reviews emerging frameworks—such as "procedural secularism" and "religious literacy"—that allow for the persistence of religious pluralism as a public reality. Ultimately, understanding the "soul" of New Labour requires us to move beyond the comic and the speculative to interrogate how religious reasoning continues to shape political power in a post-secular world.
